content.full_text:
July 18, 1978

Miss Muriel A. Foster
Treasurer Pro-Tem
California State Central Committee
of the American Party
5738 Woodleigh Drive
Carmichael, California 95608

Dear Miss Foster:

This letter will confirm our telephone conversation of July 15, 1978.
You stated at that time that the American Party of California had
sponsored a fundraising dinner for Proposition 13 on May 13, 1978.
You also stated that a committee for Special Events (a subcommittee
composed of members of your party) received all funds designated
for the dinner and made all payments for expenses incurred in con-
nection with the dinner. Additionally, any net income which may
have been realized from the sale of the tickets for the dinner was
retained by the committee. You have asked whether you, as treasurer
for the American Party, have an obligation to file as a committee
under the Political Reform Act due to your sponsorship of this event.

Government Code Section 82013 defines a Committee in relevant part as:

"...any person or combination of persons who
receives contributions or makes expenditures or
contributions for the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the action of the voters
for or against the nomination or election of one
or more candidates, or the passage or defeat of
any measure, including any committee or subcom-
mittee of a political party, whether national
state or local, if:

(a) Contributions received total five hundred
dollars ($500) or more in a calendar year."

Government Code Section 84100 requires that every "person"1/ which
qualifies as a Recipient Committee as a result of receiving $500 or
more in a calendar year must have a treasurer who has an obligation
to register with the Secretary of State on behalf of the committee.

1/Government Code Section 82047: "Person" means an individual, pro-
prietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business
trust, company, corporation, association, committee, and any other
organization or group of persons acting in concert.

Miss Muriel A. Foster                    -2-                    July 18, 1978


If you, as treasurer of the American Party, do not receive an account-
ing of the funds received or spent by the Committee for Special Events
chaired by Wilma Shirley the Party need not register as a committee
under the law simply because it sponsored the event. If the Committee
for Special Events, however, chooses to provide you with an accounting
and other substantiating records pertaining to the event, the Committee
for Special Events may then assume the role of the Party's "agent"?/
and the Party will therefore assume the reporting obligation. If the
Committee for Special Events is not "agent" of the Party, they may quali-
fy as a recipient committee (under Government Code Section 82013(a))
with an obligation to register with the Secretary of State's Office
and file campaign statements.

A failure to report information required by the law may make the non-
reporting entity subject to fines or other penalties as outlined in
the Political Reform Act.

Very truly yours,


Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Technical Assistance Program
Coordinator




Z/Government Code Section 84100(a) in relevant part: "All contributions
received by a person acting as an agent of a committee shall be reported
promptly by the recipient to the committee's treasurer or any of his
designated agents. "Promptly" as used in this section means not later
than five days before the closing date of any campaign statement required
to be filed by the treasurer, and immediately if the contribution was
received less than five days before the closing date. All contributions
shall be segregated from and may not be commingled with any personal
funds of the recipient or any other person."




cc: Marie A. Shirley, State Chairman

P   P  .

July 18, 1978

Miss Muriel A. Foster
Treasurer Pro-Tem
California State Central Committee
of the American Party
5738 Woodleigh Drive
Carmichael, California 95608

Dear Miss Foster:

This letter will confirm our telephone conversation of July 15, 1978.
You stated at that time that the American Party of California had
sponsored a fundraising dinner for Proposition 13 on May 13, 1978.
You also stated that a Committee for Special Events (a subcommittee
composed of members of your party) received all funds designated
for the dinner and made all payments for expenses incurred in connection with the dinner. Additionally, any net income which may
have been realized from the sale of the tickets for the dinner was
retained by the committee. You have asked whether you, as treasurer
for the American Party, have an obligation to file as a committee
under the Political Reform Act due to your sponsorship of this event.

Government Code Section 82013 defines a Committee in relevant part as:

    "...any person or combination of persons who
    receives contributions or makes expenditures or
    contributions for the purpose of influencing or
    attempting to influence the action of the voters
    for or against the nomination or election of one
    or more candidates, or the passage or defeat of
    any measure, including any committee or subcommittee of a political party, whether national
    state or local, if:

    (a) Contributions received total five hundred
        dollars ($500) or more in a calendar year."

Government Code Section 84100 requires that every "person"1/ which
qualifies as a Recipient Committee as a result of receiving $500 or
more in a calendar year must have a treasurer who has an obligation
to register with the Secretary of State on behalf of the committee.

1/Government Code Section 82047: "Person" means an individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business
trust, company, corporation, association, committee, and any other
organization or group of persons acting in concert.

Miss Muriel A. Foster                    -2-                    July 18, 1978


If you, as treasurer of the American Party, do not receive an account-
ing of the funds received or spent by the Committee for Special Events
chaired by Wilma Shirley the Party need not report a committee
under the law simply because it sponsored the event. If the Committee
for Special Events, however, chooses to provide you with an accounting
and other substantiating records pertaining to the event, the Committee
for Special Events may then assume the role of the Party's "agent"2/
and the Party will therefore assume the reporting obligation. If the
Committee for Special Events is not an "agent" of the Party, they may quali-
fy as a recipient committee (under Government Code Section 82013(a))
with an obligation to register with the Secretary of State's Office
and file campaign statements.

A failure to report information required by the law may make the non-
reporting entity subject to fines or other penalties as outlined in
the Political Reform Act.

Very truly yours,


Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Technical Assistance Program
     Coordinator


_________________________________________________________________
2/Government Code Section 84100(c) in relevant part: "All contributions
received by a person acting as an agent of a committee shall be reported
promptly by the recipient to the committee's treasurer or any of his
designated agents. "Promptly" as used in this section means not later
than five days before the closing date of any campaign statement required
to be filed by the treasurer, and immediately if the contribution was
received less than five days before the closing date. All contributions
shall be segregated from and may not be commingled with any personal
funds of the recipient or any other person."




cc: Maxie A. Shirley, State Chairman

Office of the Secretary of State | 111 Capitol Mall, Room 220 | Elections Division
March Fong Eu | Sacramento, California, 95814 | (916) 445-0620

JUL 2 7 1978

June 29,1978


Miss Muriel A. Foster
Treasurer Pro-Tem
California State Central
Committee of the American Party
5738 Woodleigh Drive
Carmichael, CA 95608

Dear Miss Foster:

Thank you for your letter of June 22 concerning political
body sponsorship of a fund raising dinner in connection with
a statewide initiative petition.

There is no restriction on whether a political body can
sponsor fund raising events for political purposes, and no
requirement that a political body file papers requesting
approval of the event with a public agency. Therefore, there
appears to be no question of the legality of holding the
event referred to in your letter.

There is, however, a question of whether the sponsors of the
event have incurred reporting duties under the Political Reform
Act of 1974. The money appears to have been raised in support
or opposition to a statewide initiative measure. Government
Code 82013(a) defines a "Recipient committee" that incurs
reporting duties as one whose "contributions received total
five hundred dollars or more in a calendar year."

The issue at this point involves questions of whether a
reporting duty has been incurred, and if so, which body has
the duty of reporting (is it the "American Party" or the
"Committee for Special Event"), and what filing deadlines
apply.

Miss Muriel A. Foster
Page 2
June 29, 1978


I am sending a copy of your letter to the Technical
Assistance Division of the Fair Political Practices
Commission, which will be able to assist you with potential
reporting requirements.

Very truly yours,

WILLIAM N. DURLEY
Assistant to the Secretary of State
Elections and Political Reform

[signature]

RICHARD B. MANESS
Legal Counsel
Elections and Political Reform

RBM:dk

the American Party                          CALIFORNIA STATE CENTRAL COMMITTEE OF
                                                     the American Party
                                                     P.O. Box 41562 • Sacramento, California 95841
                                                     (916) 483-4653

June 22, 1978


Secretary of State
Elections Division
111 Capitol Mall
Sacramento, California 95814

ATTN: Mr. Maness
      Elections Counsel

Dear Mr. Maness:

This is to confirm my telephone call this date for an appointment
regarding a clarification on the matter of a political body and/or
party sponsoring a fund raising event in support of an initiative
measure.

Enclosed for the record is a copy of the flyer issued for this
purpose.

On May 8, 1978 I called Mr. Arnold's Office on this and was referred
to the Elections Division and subsequently to someone in your office.
The information given me at that time on the phone was in effect,
"Where is nothing in the Elections Reform Act making it illegal for
a political party to sponsor a fund raising event in support of an
Initiative Measure. However, it is required that the Committee in
charge of the event must file a report with the Secretary of State as
to the amount of the funds raised when the amount is over $ 500.00".

Your cooperation in providing this information in writing is requested
as the authority needed for the Committee's compliance as a record
for our files and will be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

Sincerely yours,

Muriel A. Foster
Muriel A. Foster-(Miss)
Treasurer Pro-Tem
California State Central Committee
of the American Party

Encl: Dinner Flyer

the American Party

CALIFORNIA STATE CENTRAL COMMITTEE OF
the American Party
MAIL-O-GRAM

P.O. Box 41562 • Sacramento, California 95841 • (916) 483-4653

AMERICAN PARTY OF CALIFORNIA SPONSORS FUND-RAISING DINNER
FOR PROPOSITION 13
Senator Hotel Empire Room
12th and L Streets, Sacramento, CA 95814
Phone (916) 442-5081

Honoring

HOWARD JARVIS          PAUL GANN

GUEST SPEAKERS

Saturday Evening, May 13, 1978
No-Host Cocktail Hour — 6:30 p.m.
Prime Rib Dinner — 7:30 p.m.

COME HAVE FUN AND HEAR THE TRUE FACTS ON PROPOSITION 13!
Donation $25

DINNER RESERVATIONS MUST BE POSTMARKED NO LATER THAN MAY 2, 1978

Six Floors of Parking at 10th and L

BECAUSE OF THE POPULARITY OF PROPOSITION 13 AND ITS AUTHORS, SEATING IS EXTREMELY LIMITED.
SEND FOR YOUR RESERVATIONS TODAY!

DETACH AND MAIL TO:

.....................................................................................................................................................................................................................................

Wilma Shirley, Chairman
Committee for Special Events
2504 Hernando Road, Sacramento, Ca 95825

(Make checks payable to Committee for Special Event)

Enclosed $__________ donation ($25 donation per person) for ______________ reservations for AMERICAN
PARTY Dinner featuring Howard Jarvis and Paul Gann as honored speakers.

Name __________________________________________________________ Phone (    ) __________________

Address _________________________________________________ City _________________________________

County __________________________________________________________________________ Zip ________

(I understand I can pick up my tickets at the door)

I cannot attend, however, enclosed is $__________ to help assure the success of Proposition 13.

Make donation checks payable and send to:
PEOPLES ADVOCATE, c/o P.O. Box 41562, Sacramento, CA 95841

sections.question: You have asked whether you, as treasurer for the American Party, have an obligation to file as a committee under the Political Reform Act due to your sponsorship of this event.
sections.conclusion: If you, as treasurer of the American Party, do not receive an accounting of the funds received or spent by the Committee for Special Events chaired by Wilma Shirley the Party need not register as a committee under the law simply because it sponsored the event. If the Committee for Special Events, however, chooses to provide you with an accounting and other substantiating records pertaining to the event, the Committee for Special Events may then assume the role of the Party's "agent" and the party will therefore assume the reporting obligation. If the Committee for Special Events is not "agent" of the Party, they may qualify as a recipient committee (under Government Code Section 82013(a)) with an obligation to register with the Secretary of State's Office and file campaign statements.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the American Party, as treasurer, must register as a committee under the Political Reform Act when sponsoring a fundraising dinner for Proposition 13 where a subcommittee (Committee for Special Events) receives and manages all funds.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Registration obligation depends on whether the American Party receives accounting from the Committee for Special Events. If no accounting is provided, the Party need not register. If accounting is provided, the Party assumes reporting obligations as the Committee becomes its agent; alternatively, the Committee for Special Events may independently qualify as a recipient committee with separate filing obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple duplicate pages and handwritten dates. Cover letter from Secretary of State Elections Division (June 29, 1978) and initial inquiry letter (June 22, 1978) are included before the main FPPC advice letter dated July 18, 1978. Minor OCR errors present but main Q/A sections clearly identifiable. Date discrepancy: letterhead shows 'July 18, 1919' but context indicates 1978.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked whether you, as treasurer for the American Party, have an obligation to file as a committee under the Political Reform Act due to your sponsorship of this event.

CONCLUSION: If you, as treasurer of the American Party, do not receive an accounting of the funds received or spent by the Committee for Special Events chaired by Wilma Shirley the Party need not register as a committee under the law simply because it sponsored the event. If the Committee for Special Events, however, chooses to provide you with an accounting and other substantiating records pertaining to the event, the Committee for Special Events may then assume the role of the Party's "agent" and the party will therefore assume the reporting obligation. If the Committee for Special Events is not "agent" of the Party, they may qualify as a recipient committee (under Government Code Section 82013(a)) with an obligation to register with the Secretary of State's Office and file campaign statements.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether the American Party must register as a committee for sponsoring a Proposition 13 fundraising dinner managed by its subcommittee (Committee for Special Events). The registration requirement depends on whether the Party receives accounting records from the subcommittee, determining whether an agent relationship exists.
